title.zh: 卷五十六 董仲舒傳
title.raw: 卷五十六 董仲舒傳 Volume 56: Dong Zhongshu

sentences.zh: 卷五十六 董仲舒傳第二十六

translations.literal: Volume 56: Biography of Dong Zhongshu, No. 26
translations.idiomatic: Book 56: The Biography of Dong Zhongshu, the Twenty-Sixth

sentences.zh: 董仲舒，廣川人也。

translations.literal: Dong Zhongshu was a man of Guangchuan.
translations.idiomatic: Dong Zhongshu came from Guangchuan.

sentences.zh: 少治《春秋》，孝景時為博士。

translations.literal: In youth he studied the Chunqiu; in the time of Emperor Xiaojing he became an Academician.
translations.idiomatic: He had devoted himself to the Spring and Autumn Annals from an early age, and under Emperor Jing he was appointed an erudite.

sentences.zh: 下帷講誦，弟子傳以久次相授業，或莫見其面。

translations.literal: He let down the curtain and lectured and recited; disciples passed on instruction by seniority of tenure, and some never saw his face.
translations.idiomatic: He drew the curtains and taught, passing students on through successive cohorts so that some never met him face to face.

sentences.zh: 蓋三年不窺園，其精如此。

translations.literal: Thus for three years he did not glance at the garden—such was his concentration.
translations.idiomatic: They say he did not so much as look at his garden for three years; such was the depth of his focus.

sentences.zh: 進退容止，非禮不行，學士皆師尊之。

translations.literal: In advancing and withdrawing, in bearing and demeanor, if it was not according to ritual he did not act; scholars all took him as teacher and honored him.
translations.idiomatic: In every step he took and every gesture he made, he would not move outside the bounds of ritual, and the literati revered him as their master.

sentences.zh: 武帝即位，舉賢良文學之士前後百數，而仲舒以賢良對策焉。

translations.literal: When Emperor Wu took the throne, worthy and cultivated men were recommended by the hundreds before and after, and Zhongshu answered the policy questions as one recommended for worthiness.
translations.idiomatic: After Emperor Wu came to the throne, he summoned hundreds of cultivated worthies in succession, and Dong Zhongshu was among those who answered the throne’s policy questions as a recommended talent.

sentences.zh: 制曰：「朕獲承至尊休德，傳之亡窮，而施之罔極，任大而守重，是以夙夜不皇康寧，永惟萬事之統，猶懼有闕。

translations.literal: The edict said: “I have received the supreme position and its blessed virtue, to transmit it without end and apply it without limit; the burden I bear is heavy and the charge I keep is weighty, so day and night I find no ease or repose, and ever ponder the thread of the myriad affairs, yet still fear there are gaps.”
translations.idiomatic: The emperor’s rescript ran: “Having inherited the highest seat and its gracious power, I would extend it endlessly and apply it without bound; the task is vast and the stewardship weighty, so I am never wholly at ease, pondering how all things hang together and still dreading where I may fall short.”

sentences.zh: 故廣延四方之豪俊，郡國諸侯公選賢良修潔博習之士，欲聞大道之要，至論之極。

translations.literal: Therefore I have broadly summoned the outstanding from the four quarters, and commandery, kingdom, and marquisate authorities to select worthies who are cultivated, learned, and broadly read, because I desire to hear the essentials of the Great Way and the utmost of thorough discourse.
translations.idiomatic: I have therefore called in talent from every quarter and ordered the commanderies, kingdoms, and nobles to nominate cultivated scholars of wide learning, for I wish to hear the heart of the Great Way and the farthest reach of true argument.

sentences.zh: 今子大夫褎然為舉首，朕甚嘉之。

translations.literal: Now you, great officers, have stood forth as first among those raised; I greatly commend it.
translations.idiomatic: You who have been set at the head of this cohort have my warm approval.

sentences.zh: 子大夫其精心致思，朕垂聽而問焉。

translations.literal: You, great officers, should concentrate your minds and extend your thought; I hang my ear and put questions to you.”
translations.idiomatic: Apply your minds fully, for I am listening and will put questions to you.”

sentences.zh: 蓋聞五帝三王之道，改制作樂而天下洽和，百王同之。

translations.literal: I have heard that the way of the Five Thearchs and Three Kings was to reform institutions and make music, and all under Heaven was harmonized; the hundred kings shared in this.
translations.idiomatic: It is said that the Five Thearchs and the Three Kings set the world in order by changing institutions and composing ritual music, and that every true king has walked the same path.

sentences.zh: 當虞氏之樂莫盛於《韶》，於周莫盛於《勺》。

translations.literal: In the time of the Yu clan no music was more splendid than the Shao; in Zhou none was more splendid than the Zhuo.
translations.idiomatic: Under the house of Yu nothing surpassed the Shao; under the Zhou nothing surpassed the Shao in its Zhou form.

sentences.zh: 聖王已沒，鐘鼓管弦之聲未衰，而大道微缺，陵夷至乎桀、紂之行，王道大壞矣。

translations.literal: The sage-kings have passed away, yet the sounds of bells, drums, strings, and pipes have not declined, while the Great Way has grown faint and flawed, sliding down to the conduct of Jie and Zhou Xin, and the kingly way was greatly ruined.
translations.idiomatic: The sage-kings are gone, but pipes and strings still sound, even as the great Way has thinned and cracked, sliding all the way to the tyranny of Jie and Zhou Xin, when the royal order collapsed.

sentences.zh: 夫五百年之間，守文之君，當塗之士，欲則先王之法以戴翼其世者甚眾，然猶不能反，日以僕滅，至後王而後止，豈其所持操或誖繆而失其統與？

translations.literal: Within those five hundred years, rulers who guarded the written tradition and men of power who wished to take the laws of the former kings to support their age were very many, yet still they could not turn things back, daily collapsing, until it stopped with later kings—is it that what they grasped and pursued was sometimes perverse and mistaken and they lost the thread?
translations.idiomatic: Across those five hundred years, conservative emperors and powerful ministers who tried to prop up their times with the models of antiquity were legion, yet the fall could not be reversed until it ran its course in later reigns—was it because their programs were skewed and they lost the true thread?

sentences.zh: 固天降命不查復反，必推之於大衰而後息與？

translations.literal: Is it indeed that Heaven’s ordained mandate cannot again return, and must be pushed to utter decline before it rests?
translations.idiomatic: Or is Heaven’s decree fixed, never to swing back until ruin has run its course?

sentences.zh: 烏乎！

translations.literal: Wuhu!
translations.idiomatic: Alas!

sentences.zh: 凡所為屑屑，夙興夜寐，務法上古者，又將無補與？

translations.literal: All that anxious toil, rising early and sleeping late, striving to model the high antiquity—will it again be of no help?
translations.idiomatic: All that scrupulous labor, rising before dawn and retiring late, straining to imitate high antiquity—does any of it truly mend what is broken?

sentences.zh: 三代受命，其符安在？

translations.literal: When the Three Dynasties received the mandate, where lay their validating signs?
translations.idiomatic: When the three dynasties received Heaven’s charge, what omens confirmed them?

sentences.zh: 災異之變，何緣而起？

translations.literal: By what cause do the changes of disasters and prodigies arise?
translations.idiomatic: What brings about calamities and strange portents?

sentences.zh: 性命之情，或夭或壽，或仁或鄙，習聞其號，未燭厥理。

translations.literal: As for the disposition of nature and fate—some short-lived, some long-lived, some humane, some base—I have long heard their names but have not illumined their principles.
translations.idiomatic: Some men die young and some live long; some are humane and some coarse—I know the labels, yet their inner pattern stays dark to me.

sentences.zh: 伊欲風流而令行，刑輕而奸改，百姓和樂，政事宣昭，何修何飭而膏露降，百谷登，德潤四海，澤臻草木，三光全，寒暑平，受天之祜，享鬼神之靈，德澤洋溢，施乎方外，延及群生？

translations.literal: What must I cultivate and set in order so that customs will flow and commands will be obeyed, punishments will be light yet wickedness will reform, the hundred surnames will be harmonious and glad, and government will shine forth clearly—so that sweet dew will descend, the hundred grains ripen, virtue will moisten the four seas, bounty will reach plants and trees, the three luminaries will be whole, cold and heat will be balanced, I will receive Heaven’s blessing and enjoy the spirits’ favor, overflowing virtue will extend beyond the borders, and reach all living things?”
translations.idiomatic: What should I refine and regulate so that manners improve and orders are followed, penalties stay mild yet crime turns aside, the people live at peace, and policy stands clear—until sweet dew falls, grain fills the bins, virtue soaks the realm, grace touches every blade of grass, sun, moon, and stars keep their courses, the seasons stay true, Heaven’s favor rests on me, the spirits lend their power, and beneficence spreads beyond the frontiers to every creature?”

sentences.zh: 子大夫明先聖之業，習俗化之變，終始之序，講聞高誼之日久矣，其明以諭朕。

translations.literal: You, great officers, understand the undertakings of the former sages, the changes wrought by custom and transformation, and the sequence of beginning and end; you have long listened to and heard their lofty meaning—make it clear to explain to me.
translations.idiomatic: You are steeped in the legacy of the sages, in how custom reshapes the world, and in how beginnings lead to ends; you have long pondered their high teaching—now set it out plainly for me.

sentences.zh: 科別其條，勿猥勿並，取之於術，慎其所出。

translations.literal: Distinguish your points by category; do not crowd or conflate them; take them from method, and be careful about what you advance.
translations.idiomatic: Sort your answers into clear topics; do not ramble or lump unlike things together; ground what you say in a coherent method and weigh every claim you put forward.

sentences.zh: 乃其不正不直，不忠不極，枉於執事，書之不洩，興於朕躬，毋悼後害。

translations.literal: If there is what is not straight, not correct, not loyal, not utmost, and you twist the duties you hold, what is written will not be leaked; if it arises from my person, do not fear later harm.
translations.idiomatic: If anything in your replies is crooked, disloyal, or less than fully candid, or if you twist the trust of office, the record will stay sealed; if fault lies with me, speak without dread of later blame.”

sentences.zh: 子大夫其盡心，靡有所隱，朕將親覽焉。

translations.literal: You, great officers, exhaust your minds and hide nothing; I will personally review it.”
translations.idiomatic: Give your fullest counsel and conceal nothing; I shall read it with my own eyes.”

sentences.zh: 仲舒對曰：

translations.literal: Zhongshu replied, saying:
translations.idiomatic: Dong Zhongshu answered:

sentences.zh: 陛下發德音，下明詔，求天命與情性，皆非愚臣之所能及也。

translations.literal: Your Majesty has issued virtuous words and sent down clear edicts, seeking Heaven’s mandate and the disposition of nature and feeling—none of this is what a foolish minister can reach.
translations.idiomatic: The virtue in your tone and the clarity of your edict, asking after Heaven’s command and the truth of human nature and passion—these lie beyond the reach of a humble official like myself.

sentences.zh: 臣謹案《春秋》之中，視前世已行之事，以觀天人相與之際，甚可畏也。

translations.literal: Your servant respectfully examines within the Chunqiu the deeds already practiced in former ages, to observe the juncture where Heaven and man join one another—it is very awesome.
translations.idiomatic: I have combed the Spring and Autumn Annals for what past ages actually did, to see how Heaven and humanity meet—and the sight is sobering.

sentences.zh: 國家將有失道之敗，而天乃先出災害以譴告之，不知自省，又出怪異以警懼之，尚不知變，而傷敗乃至。

translations.literal: When the state is about to have the defeat of losing the Way, Heaven first sends disasters to reprimand and inform it; if it does not know to examine itself, Heaven again sends prodigies to awe and frighten it; if still it does not know to change, then injury and ruin arrive.
translations.idiomatic: When a dynasty is sliding toward the ruin that follows from abandoning the Way, Heaven first sends disasters as a rebuke; if the throne does not look inward, stranger portents follow to rouse fear; if still there is no reform, ruin closes in.

sentences.zh: 以此見天心之仁愛人君而欲止其亂也。

translations.literal: By this is seen that the mind of Heaven is benevolent in cherishing the lord of men and desires to stop his disorder.
translations.idiomatic: This shows Heaven’s heart: it loves the ruler and wants to halt his disorder before it is too late.

sentences.zh: 自非大亡道之世者，天盡欲扶持而全安之，事在強勉而已矣。

translations.literal: Unless it is an age of great loss of the Way, Heaven wholly desires to support and secure him; the matter lies in forceful effort, that is all.
translations.idiomatic: For any age short of utter moral collapse, Heaven wishes to uphold the sovereign and keep him whole; everything turns on resolute effort.

sentences.zh: 強勉學習，則聞見博而知益明；

translations.literal: If one forcefully strives at learning, then what is heard and seen is broad and knowledge becomes daily clearer;
translations.idiomatic: Press yourself in study, and hearing and sight widen while understanding grows clearer day by day;

sentences.zh: 強勉行道，則德日起而大有功：此皆可使還至而有效者也。

translations.literal: if one forcefully strives at walking the Way, then virtue rises daily and there is great achievement: these are all things that can be turned back to arrive and have effect.
translations.idiomatic: press yourself in practicing the Way, and character mounts while great deeds accumulate—both can be turned around quickly and will show real results.

sentences.zh: 《詩》曰「夙夜匪解」，《書》云「茂哉茂哉！」

translations.literal: The Odes say “Early and late without slackening”; the Documents say “Splendid indeed, splendid indeed!”
translations.idiomatic: The Classic of Poetry says, “From dawn to dusk he never slackens”; the Documents cry, “How glorious, how glorious!”

sentences.zh: 皆強勉之謂也。

translations.literal: These all speak of forceful effort.
translations.idiomatic: Both lines praise unwearying exertion.

sentences.zh: 道者，所繇適於治之路也，仁義禮樂皆其具也。

translations.literal: The Way is the road by which one proceeds to good order; benevolence, righteousness, ritual, and music are all its implements.
translations.idiomatic: The Way is the path that leads to good government; ritual, music, benevolence, and righteousness are the tools found along that road.

sentences.zh: 故聖王已沒，而子孫長久安寧數百歲，此皆禮樂教化之功也。

translations.literal: Therefore when the sage-kings have passed away, yet their descendants enjoy long peace and security for hundreds of years, this is all the achievement of ritual, music, and transformation through instruction.
translations.idiomatic: That is why, long after the sage-kings are dust, their heirs can still reign in quiet for centuries—such is the work of rites, music, and moral education.

sentences.zh: 王者未作樂之時，乃用先五之樂宜於世者，而以深入教化於民。

translations.literal: When the king has not yet made music, then he uses the music of former kings that suits the age, in order deeply to transform the people through instruction.
translations.idiomatic: Before a new king composes his own hymns, he adopts the court music handed down from earlier rulers that fits his times, and with it sinks moral teaching deep among the people.

sentences.zh: 教化之情不得，雅頌之樂不成，故王者功成作樂，樂其德也。

translations.literal: If the disposition of transformation through instruction is not obtained, the music of the Ya and the Song is not completed; therefore when the king’s merit is accomplished he makes music, rejoicing in his virtue.
translations.idiomatic: Until the temper of that instruction is right, the great court hymns cannot be finished; so the true king, his task complete, then fashions music to celebrate his inner power.

sentences.zh: 樂者，所以變民風，化民俗也；

translations.literal: Music is that by which one changes the people’s customs and transforms the people’s habits;
translations.idiomatic: Music exists to shift popular custom and reshape everyday habit;

sentences.zh: 其變民也易，其化人也著。

translations.literal: to change the people is easy; to transform people is manifest.
translations.idiomatic: changing surface manners is quick work; changing the human heart shows clear and lasting effect.

sentences.zh: 故聲發於和而本於情，接於肌膚，臧於骨髓。

translations.literal: Therefore sound issues from harmony and is rooted in feeling; it touches the skin and is stored in bone and marrow.
translations.idiomatic: Sound arises from harmony and takes root in emotion; it meets the skin and lodges in the marrow.

sentences.zh: 故王道雖微缺，而管弦之聲未衰也。

translations.literal: Therefore although the kingly way may be slightly flawed, the sounds of strings and pipes have not declined.
translations.idiomatic: Hence, even when the royal order frays a little, pipes and strings still do not fall silent.

sentences.zh: 夫虞氏之不為政久矣，然而樂頌遺風猶有存者，是以孔子在齊而聞《韶》也。

translations.literal: The Yu clan has long not governed, yet the lingering air of their praise-songs still survives; therefore Confucius, in Qi, heard the Shao.
translations.idiomatic: The house of Yu has not ruled for ages, yet echoes of its ritual songs remain—which is why Confucius could hear the Shao in Qi.

sentences.zh: 夫人君莫不欲安存而惡危亡，然而政亂國危者甚眾，所任者非其人，而所繇者非其道，是以政日以僕滅也。

translations.literal: There is no lord of men who does not desire security and existence and hates danger and loss, yet those whose government is chaotic and state endangered are very many—those they employ are not the right men, and the path they follow is not the right Way; therefore government daily collapses.
translations.idiomatic: No ruler wants peril instead of peace, yet legions of them bring turmoil and danger—because they trust the wrong men and walk the wrong path, and so their rule slides steadily toward ruin.

sentences.zh: 夫周道衰於幽、厲，非道亡也，幽、厲不繇也。

translations.literal: The Zhou way declined under You and Li; it was not that the Way perished, but that You and Li did not follow it.
translations.idiomatic: The Zhou decayed under Kings You and Li not because the Way vanished, but because those two kings refused to walk in it.

sentences.zh: 至於宣王，思昔先王之德，興滯補弊，明文、武之功業，周道粲然復興，詩人美之而作，上天晁之，為生賢佐，後世稱通，至今不絕。

translations.literal: As for King Xuan, he pondered the virtue of the former kings, raised what was stagnant and repaired what was flawed, made clear the merit of Wen and Wu; the Zhou way brightly revived, the poets praised it and composed; High Heaven shone upon him, and for him produced worthy assistants; later ages called it a thoroughfare, down to the present without ceasing.
translations.idiomatic: King Xuan looked back to the virtue of his forebears, cleared blockages and patched abuses, and brought Wen and Wu’s achievement back into light; the royal road blazed anew, poets sang his praise, Heaven showed its favor, and wise ministers appeared at his side—later generations still call his reign a high road that has never been forgotten.

sentences.zh: 此夙夜不解行善之所致也。

translations.literal: This is what was brought about by not slackening day or night in doing good.
translations.idiomatic: That is what comes of never tiring, day or night, in doing the good.

sentences.zh: 孔子曰「人能弘道，非道弘人」也。

translations.literal: Confucius said, “It is man who can enlarge the Way; it is not the Way that enlarges man.”
translations.idiomatic: Confucius said, “Men broaden the Way; the Way does not broaden men.”

sentences.zh: 故治亂廢興在於己，非天降命不得可反，其所操持誖謬失其統也。

translations.literal: Therefore order and chaos, abandonment and rise, lie in oneself; it is not that Heaven sends down a mandate that cannot be turned back, but that what is grasped and pursued is perverse and mistaken and loses the thread.
translations.idiomatic: Order and disorder, ruin and revival, rest in the ruler himself; Heaven does not hand down an irreversible doom—only when he clutches the wrong program and drops the true thread does the mandate slip away.

sentences.zh: 臣聞天之所大奉使之王者，必有非人力所能致而自至者，此受命之符也。

translations.literal: Your servant has heard that what Heaven greatly grants to make a king must include what cannot be brought about by human power yet comes of itself—this is the sign of receiving the mandate.
translations.idiomatic: I have heard that when Heaven intends a man for the throne, it sends tokens no mortal contrives—such are the omens of received mandate.

sentences.zh: 天下之人同心歸之，若歸父母，故天瑞應誠而至。

translations.literal: The people of the world with one heart turn to him as to father and mother; therefore Heaven’s auspicious responses arrive in truth.
translations.idiomatic: All under Heaven then turn to him with one heart, as children to parents, and Heaven’s favorable portents answer in good faith.

sentences.zh: 《書》曰「白魚入於王舟，有火復於王屋，流為烏」，此蓋受命之符也。

translations.literal: The Documents say, “The white fish entered the king’s boat; there was fire that returned to the king’s house and flowed to become a crow”—this is probably the sign of receiving the mandate.
translations.idiomatic: The Documents record, “A white fish leaped into the king’s barge; fire ran over the royal hall and shaped itself into a crow”—such, in outline, are tokens of Heaven’s choice.

sentences.zh: 周公曰「復哉復哉」，孔子曰「德不孤，必有鄰」，皆積善累德之效也。

translations.literal: The Duke of Zhou said “Return, return”; Confucius said, “Virtue is not left alone; it always has neighbors”—these are all the effects of accumulated goodness piled up in virtue.
translations.idiomatic: The Duke of Zhou cried, “Report back, report back!” Confucius said, “Virtue never stands alone; it has neighbors”—both speak of goodness stacked high until it moves Heaven.

sentences.zh: 及至後世，淫佚衰微，不能統理群生，諸侯背畔，殘賤良民以爭壤土，廢德教而任刑罰。

translations.literal: Reaching later generations, dissipation and decline, unable to rule and bring order to the throng of the living, the marquises turned their backs, mutilated and abused the good people to fight over soil, abandoned virtuous instruction and relied on punishments.
translations.idiomatic: Later ages sank into excess and weakness: rulers could not shepherd the living, lords rebelled, butchered the innocent for land, cast aside moral teaching, and trusted the lash alone.

sentences.zh: 刑罰不中，則生邪氣；

translations.literal: When punishments are not centered, there arises perverse qi;
translations.idiomatic: When penalties miss the mean, a miasma of wrong rises;

sentences.zh: 邪氣積於下，怨惡畜於上。

translations.literal: perverse qi accumulates below, and resentment and hatred store up above.
translations.idiomatic: that foul vapor pools below while resentment gathers above;

sentences.zh: 上下不和，則陰陽繆戾而妖孽生矣。

translations.literal: when above and below are not harmonious, then yin and yang are twisted awry and monstrous prodigies are born.
translations.idiomatic: when high and low fall out of tune, yin and yang jar against each other and uncanny prodigies appear.

sentences.zh: 此災異所緣起也。

translations.literal: This is what disasters and prodigies follow from.
translations.idiomatic: That is the root from which calamities and omens spring.

sentences.zh: 臣聞命者天之令也，性者生之質也，情者人之欲也。

translations.literal: Your servant has heard that fate is Heaven’s command, nature is the stuff of life, and feeling is man’s desire.
translations.idiomatic: I take fate to be Heaven’s order, nature to be the raw stuff of life, and emotion to be human appetite.

sentences.zh: 或夭或壽，或仁或鄙，陶冶而成之，不能粹美，有治亂之所在，故不齊也。

translations.literal: Some short-lived, some long-lived, some humane, some base—the potter smelts and completes them, and they cannot be pure and perfect; there is a place where order and chaos lie, therefore they are not uniform.
translations.idiomatic: Some die young, some old; some grow humane, some coarse—the world works them like clay in a kiln, so few come out flawless; where government stands or falls, there character sorts unevenly.

sentences.zh: 孔子曰：「君子之德風，小人之德草，草上之風必偃。」

translations.literal: Confucius said: “The virtue of the gentleman is wind; the virtue of the small man is grass; when the wind passes over the grass, the grass surely bends.”
translations.idiomatic: Confucius said, “The gentleman’s virtue is wind; the commoner’s virtue is grass—let the wind sweep the grass and the grass must bend.”

sentences.zh: 故堯、舜行德則民仁壽，桀、紂行暴則民鄙夭。

translations.literal: Therefore when Yao and Shun practiced virtue the people were humane and long-lived; when Jie and Zhou Xin practiced cruelty the people were base and short-lived.
translations.idiomatic: Under Yao and Shun’s virtue the people grew humane and long-lived; under Jie and Zhou Xin’s cruelty they turned coarse and died young.

sentences.zh: 夫上之化下，下之從上，猶泥之在鈞，唯甄者之所為，猶金之在熔，唯冶者之所鑄。

translations.literal: As for the transformation from above and the following from below, it is like clay on the potter’s wheel—only what the shaper does; like metal in the molten furnace—only what the smelter casts.
translations.idiomatic: What those aloft transform, those below imitate, as clay on a wheel is only what the potter shapes, or molten bronze is only what the founder casts.

sentences.zh: 「綏之斯徠，動之斯和」，此之謂也。

translations.literal: “Pacify them and they come; move them and they harmonize”—this speaks of it.
translations.idiomatic: “Calm them and they arrive; stir them and they fall into tune”—that is the truth of it.”

sentences.zh: 臣謹案《春秋》之文，求王道之端，得之於正。

translations.literal: Your servant respectfully examines the text of the Chunqiu and, seeking the beginning of the kingly way, obtains it in “the first month.”
translations.idiomatic: I have traced the language of the Spring and Autumn Annals for where the royal way begins, and I find it in the phrase “the first month.”

sentences.zh: 正次王，王次春。

translations.literal: The first month comes second to the king; the king comes second to spring.
translations.idiomatic: The word “first” stands after “king,” and “king” stands after “spring.”

sentences.zh: 春者，天之所為也；

translations.literal: Spring is what Heaven does;
translations.idiomatic: Spring is Heaven’s act;

sentences.zh: 正者，王之所為也。

translations.literal: the first month is what the king does.
translations.idiomatic: the first month is the king’s act.

sentences.zh: 其意曰，上承天之所為，而下以正其所為，正王道之端云爾。

translations.literal: Its meaning says: above he receives what Heaven does, and below he corrects what he does—the beginning of correcting the kingly way is thus.
translations.idiomatic: The sense is: on high he takes up Heaven’s work, and below he sets his own conduct right—there lies the starting point of the true king’s path.

sentences.zh: 然則王者欲有所為，宜求其端於天。

translations.literal: Then if the king desires to do something, he should seek its beginning in Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: Therefore whatever a king would undertake, he should look for its source in Heaven’s pattern.

sentences.zh: 天道之大者在陰陽。

translations.literal: The greatest thing in Heaven’s Way lies in yin and yang.
translations.idiomatic: The chief pattern of Heaven is yin and yang.

sentences.zh: 陽為德，陰為刑；

translations.literal: Yang is virtue; yin is punishment;
translations.idiomatic: Yang stands for life-giving power; yin for killing power;

sentences.zh: 刑主殺而德主生。

translations.literal: punishment presides over killing and virtue presides over giving life.
translations.idiomatic: punishment kills while virtue nurtures.

sentences.zh: 是故陽常居大夏，而以生育養長為事；

translations.literal: Therefore yang constantly resides in great summer and takes nurturing growth as its task;
translations.idiomatic: So yang dwells in the height of summer and busies itself with fostering life;

sentences.zh: 陰常居大冬，而積於空虛不用之處。

translations.literal: yin constantly resides in great winter and piles up in empty unused places.
translations.idiomatic: yin lodges in the depth of winter and hoards itself in hollow, dormant places.

sentences.zh: 以此見天之任德不任刑也。

translations.literal: By this is seen that Heaven relies on virtue and does not rely on punishment.
translations.idiomatic: From this we see that Heaven trusts in kindness, not in the axe.

sentences.zh: 天使陽出佈施於上而主歲功，使陰入伏於下而時出佐陽；

translations.literal: Heaven causes yang to emerge and spread above and preside over the year’s work, and causes yin to withdraw and hide below and periodically emerge to assist yang;
translations.idiomatic: Heaven sends yang forth to spread above and master the year’s growth, while yin slips below and only surfaces now and then to second yang;

sentences.zh: 陽不得陰之助，亦不能獨成歲。

translations.literal: yang, without yin’s assistance, also cannot alone complete the year.
translations.idiomatic: yet without yin’s help yang cannot finish the year by itself.

sentences.zh: 終陽以成歲為名，此天意也。

translations.literal: In the end yang takes completing the year as its name—this is Heaven’s intent.
translations.idiomatic: Still, the year is named for yang’s completion—that is Heaven’s meaning.

sentences.zh: 王者承天意以從事，故任德教而不任刑。

translations.literal: The king receives Heaven’s intent to conduct affairs; therefore he relies on virtuous instruction and does not rely on punishment.
translations.idiomatic: The king takes up Heaven’s purpose, so he leans on moral teaching, not on terror.

sentences.zh: 刑者不可任以治世，猶陰之不可任以成歲也。

translations.literal: Punishment cannot be relied on to order the age, just as yin cannot be relied on to complete the year.
translations.idiomatic: You cannot govern an age on punishments any more than you can ripen a harvest on yin alone.

sentences.zh: 為政而任刑，不順於天，故先王莫之肯為也。

translations.literal: To conduct government and rely on punishment is not compliant with Heaven; therefore the former kings none of them were willing to do it.
translations.idiomatic: To rule by the rod is to defy Heaven; that is why the ancient kings would not do it.

sentences.zh: 今廢先王德教之官，而獨任執法之吏治民，毋乃任刑之意與！

translations.literal: Now you have abolished the offices of the former kings’ virtuous instruction and alone rely on clerks who wield the law to govern the people—is this not the intent to rely on punishment!
translations.idiomatic: To discard the ministers who teach goodness and leave the people to law clerks is surely to lean on the rack and rope.

sentences.zh: 孔子曰：「不教而誅謂之虐。」

translations.literal: Confucius said: “To punish without teaching is called cruelty.”
translations.idiomatic: Confucius said, “To strike before you have taught is tyranny.”

sentences.zh: 虐政用於下，而欲德教之被四海，故難成也。

translations.literal: When cruel government is applied below, yet one desires virtuous instruction to cover the four seas, therefore it is hard to accomplish.
translations.idiomatic: Cruelty below and a wish for virtue to flood the realm—those two cannot be reconciled.

sentences.zh: 臣謹案《春秋》謂一元之意，一者萬物之所從始也，元者辭之所謂大也。

translations.literal: Your servant respectfully examines the meaning in the Chunqiu that calls the first “the beginning”; the one is where the myriad things obtain their start, and the beginning is what the text calls great.
translations.idiomatic: I read in the Spring and Autumn Annals that the word “first” means “beginning”: the One is where all things take their rise, and “beginning” is the classic way of saying “greatest.”

sentences.zh: 謂一為元者，視大始而欲正本也。

translations.literal: Calling the one the beginning is looking to the great commencement and desiring to correct the root.
translations.idiomatic: To treat the One as the beginning is to gaze at creation’s first moment and set the taproot straight.

sentences.zh: 《春秋》深探其本，而反自貴者始。

translations.literal: The Chunqiu probes deeply its root, and returns to begin from what honors itself.
translations.idiomatic: The Annals bore down to the taproot and began its lesson with the ruler’s own self.

sentences.zh: 故為人君者，正心以正朝廷，正朝廷以正百官，正百官以正萬民，正萬民以正四方。

translations.literal: Therefore for the man who is lord of others, correcting the mind corrects the court; correcting the court corrects the hundred officials; correcting the hundred officials corrects the myriad people; correcting the myriad people corrects the four quarters.
translations.idiomatic: Hence the sovereign who straightens his heart straightens his court; with the court set right, the bureaucracy follows; with officials true, the people align; with the people true, the four quarters fall into line.

sentences.zh: 四方正，遠近莫敢不壹於正，而亡有邪氣奸其間者。

translations.literal: When the four quarters are correct, then far and near none dare not be unified in correctness, and there is none who with perverse qi encroaches between them.
translations.idiomatic: When the four quarters stand true, none within reach dares wander from the mean, and no foul vapor slips between ruler and ruled.

sentences.zh: 是以陰陽調而風雨時，群生和而萬民殖，五穀孰而草木茂，天地之間被潤澤而大豐美，四海之內聞盛德而皆徠臣，諸福之物，可致之祥，莫不畢至，而王道終矣。

translations.literal: Therefore yin and yang are tuned and wind and rain come in season, the throng of the living is harmonious and the myriad people multiply, the five grains ripen and plants and trees flourish, between Heaven and Earth there is moistening bounty and great abundance, within the four seas they hear the flourishing virtue and all come to be ministers; things of blessing and omens that can be brought to arrive—none fail to come in full, and the kingly way is finished.
translations.idiomatic: Then yin and yang keep time, wind and rain obey the seasons, creatures thrive and the people multiply, grain ripens and the woods grow thick, Heaven and earth share one rich damp, and within the four seas all hear the shining virtue and come to serve; every auspicious token that can answer a true king arrives at once, and the royal work is complete.

sentences.zh: 孔子曰：「鳳鳥不至，河不出圖，吾已矣夫！」

translations.literal: Confucius said: “The phoenix does not come; the river does not yield the chart—I am finished!”
translations.idiomatic: Confucius said, “No phoenix comes; the Yellow River sends up no chart—for me it is over.”

sentences.zh: 自悲可致此物，而身卑賤不得致也。

translations.literal: He grieved for himself that he could bring these things about, yet in body base and lowly could not bring them about.
translations.idiomatic: He mourned because he knew such omens could answer a sage, yet his low station barred him from summoning them.

sentences.zh: 今陛下貴為天子，富有四海，居得致之位，操可致之勢，又有能致之資，行高而恩厚，知明而意美，愛民而好士，可謂誼主矣。

translations.literal: Now Your Majesty is honored as Son of Heaven, rich in possessing the four seas, dwelling in a position where they can be brought to arrive, holding a situation where they can be brought to arrive, and also having the endowment by which they can be brought to arrive; conduct is high and grace is deep, knowledge is bright and intent is beautiful, cherishing the people and loving scholars—you can be called a lord of righteousness.
translations.idiomatic: You sit as Son of Heaven, own the world, stand where portents may answer, hold power they will heed, and possess the gifts that draw them; your conduct is lofty, your favor deep, your understanding keen, your purpose fair, you love the people and honor the learned—you are a righteous sovereign.

sentences.zh: 然而天地未應而美祥莫至者，何也？

translations.literal: Yet Heaven and Earth have not responded and fine auspices have not arrived—why?
translations.idiomatic: Yet Heaven and earth withhold their echo and no great omens come—why?

sentences.zh: 凡以教化不立而萬民不正也。

translations.literal: In all cases it is because transformation through instruction is not established and the myriad people are not correct.
translations.idiomatic: The reason, in every case, is that public teaching has not been founded, so the people never find their true north.

sentences.zh: 夫萬民之從利也，如水之走下，不以教化堤防之，不能止也。

translations.literal: As for the myriad people’s following profit, it is like water’s flowing downward; if you do not dyke it with transformation through instruction, it cannot be stopped.
translations.idiomatic: The multitude chase profit as water runs downhill; without the levees of ritual instruction nothing will check them.

sentences.zh: 是故教化立而奸邪皆止者，其堤防完也；

translations.literal: Therefore when transformation through instruction is established and treachery and deviance all stop, its dykes and dams are complete;
translations.idiomatic: When teaching is firmly set and wickedness ends, the dikes are whole;

sentences.zh: 教化廢而奸邪並出，刑罰不能勝者，其堤防壞也。
古之王者明於此，是故南面而治天下，莫不以教化為大務。
立太學以教於國，設癢序以化於邑，漸民以仁，摩民以誼，節民以禮，故其刑罰甚輕而禁不犯者，教化行而習俗美也。
聖王之繼亂世也，掃除其跡而悉去之，復修教化而崇起之。
教化已明，習俗已成，子孫循之，行五六百歲尚未敗也。
至周之末世，大為亡道，以失天下。
秦繼其後，獨不能改，又益甚之，重禁文學，不得挾書，棄捐禮誼而惡聞之，其心欲盡滅先聖之道，而顓為自恣苟簡之治，故立為天子十四歲而國破亡矣。
自古以來，未嘗有以亂濟亂，大敗天下之民如秦者也。
其遺毒余烈，至今未滅，使習俗薄惡，人民□頑，抵冒殊扞，孰爛如此之甚者也。
孔子曰：「腐朽之木不可雕也，糞土之牆不可圬也。」
今漢繼秦之後，如朽木、糞牆矣，雖欲善治之，亡可奈何。
法出而奸生，令下而詐起，如以湯止沸，抱薪救火，愈甚亡益也。
竊譬之琴瑟不調，甚者必解而更張之，乃可鼓也；
為政而不行，甚者必變而更化之，乃可理也。
當更張而不更張，雖有良工不能善調也：當更化而不更化，雖有大賢不能善治也。
故漢得天下以來，常欲善治而至今不可善治者，失之於當更化而不更化也。
古人有言曰：「臨淵羨魚，不如退而結網。」
今臨政而願治七十餘歲矣，不如退而更化；
更化則可善治，善治則災害日去，福祿日來。
《詩》云：「宜民宜人，受祿於人。」
為政而宜於民者，固當受祿於天。
夫仁、誼、禮、知、信五常之道，王者所當修飭也；
五者修飭，故受天之晁，而享鬼神之靈，德施於方外，延及群生也。
天子覽其對而異焉，乃復冊之曰：
制曰：蓋聞虞舜之時，游於巖郎之上，垂拱無為，而天下太平。
周文王至於日昃不暇食，而宇內亦治。
夫帝王之道，豈不同條共貫與？
何逸勞之殊也？
蓋儉者不造玄黃旌旗之飾。
及至周室，設兩觀，乘大路，硃干玉戚，八佾陳於庭，而頌聲興。
夫帝王之道豈異指哉？
或曰良玉不瑑，又曰非文亡以輔德，二端異焉。
殷人執五刑以督奸，傷肌膚以懲惡。
成、康不式，四十餘年天下不犯，囹圄空虛。
秦國用之，死者甚眾，刑者相望，□矣哀哉！
烏乎！
朕夙寤晨興，惟前帝王之憲，永思所以奉至尊，章洪業，皆在力本任賢。
今朕親耕籍田以為農先，勸孝弟，崇有德，使者冠蓋相望，問勤勞，恤孤獨，盡思極神，功烈休德未始雲獲也。
今陰陽錯繆，氛氣充塞，群生寡遂，黎民未濟，廉恥貿亂，賢不肖渾淆，未得其真，故詳延特起之士，庶幾乎！
今子大夫待詔百有餘人，或道世務而未濟，稽諸上古之不同，考之於今而難行，毋乃牽於文系而不得騁與？
將所繇異術，所聞殊方與？
各悉對，著於篇，毋諱有司。
明其指略，切磋究之。
以稱朕意。
仲舒對曰：
臣聞堯受命，以天下為憂，而未以位為樂也，故誅逐亂臣，務求賢聖，是以得舜、禹、稷、□、咎繇。
眾聖輔德，賢能佐職，教化大行，天下和洽，萬民皆安仁樂誼，各得其宜，動作應禮，從容中道。
故孔子曰：「如有王者，必世而後仁，」此之謂也。
堯在位七十載，乃遜於位以禪虞舜。
堯崩，天下不歸堯子丹硃而歸舜。
舜知不可辟，乃即天子之位，以禹為相，因堯之輔佐，繼其統業，是以垂拱無為而天下治。
孔子曰「《韶》盡美矣，又盡善矣」，此之謂也。
至於殷紂，逆天暴物，殺戮賢知，殘賊百姓。
伯夷、太公皆當世賢者，隱處而不為臣。
守職之人皆奔走逃亡，入於河海。
天下□亂，萬民不安，故天下去殷而從周。
文王順天理物，師用賢聖，是以閎夭、大顛、散宜生等亦聚於朝廷。
愛施兆民，天下歸之，故太公起海濱而即三公也。
當此之時，紂尚在上，尊卑昏亂，百姓散亡，故文王悼痛而欲安之，是以日昃而不暇食民。
孔子作《春秋》，先正王而系萬事，見素王之文焉。
由此觀之，帝王之條貫同，然而勞逸異者，所遇之時異也。
孔子曰「《武》盡美矣，未盡善也」，此之謂也。
臣聞制度文采玄黃之飾，所以明尊卑，異貴賤，而勸有德也。
故《春秋》受命所先制者，改正朔，易服色，所以應天也。
然則官至旌旗之制，有法而然者也。
故孔子曰：「奢則不遜，儉則固。」
儉非聖人之中制也。
臣聞良玉不□，資質潤美，不待刻□，此亡異於達巷黨人不學而自知也。
然則常玉不□，不成文章；
君子不學，不成其德。
臣聞聖王之治天下也，少則習之學，長則材諸位，爵祿以養其德，刑罰以威其惡，故民曉於禮誼而恥犯其上。
武王行大誼，平殘賊，周公作禮樂以文之，至於成康之隆，囹圄空虛四十餘年，此亦教化之漸而仁誼之流，非獨傷肌膚之效也。
至秦則不然。
師申商之法，行韓非之說，憎帝王之道，以貪狼為俗，非有文德以教訓於下也。
誅名而不察實，為善者不必免，而犯惡者未必刑也。
是以百官皆飾虛辭而不顧實，外有事君之禮，內有背上之心；
造偽飾詐，趣利無恥；
又好用□酷之吏，賦斂亡度，竭民財力，百姓散亡，不得從耕織之業，群盜並起。
是以刑者甚眾，死者相望，而奸不息，俗化使然也。
故孔子曰「導之以政，齊之以刑，民免而無恥」，此之謂也。
今陛下並有天下，海內莫不率服，廣覽兼聽，極群下之知，盡天下之美，至德昭然，施於方外。
夜郎、康居，殊方萬里，說德歸誼，此太平之致也。
然而功不加於百姓者，殆王心來加焉。
曾子曰：「尊其所聞，則高明矣；
行其所知，則光大矣。
高明光大，不在於它，在乎加之意而已。」
願陛下因用所聞，設誠於內而致行之，則三王何異哉！
陛下親耕籍田以為農先，夙寤晨興，憂勞萬民，思維往古，而務以求賢，此亦堯、舜之用心也，然而未雲獲者，士素不厲也。
夫不素養士而欲求賢，譬猶不琢玉而求文采也。
故養士之大者，莫大乎太學；
太學者，賢士之所關也，教化之本原也。
今以一郡一國之眾，對亡應書者，是王道往往而絕也。
臣願陛下興太學，置明師，以養天下之士，數考問以盡其材，則英俊宜可得矣。
今之郡守、縣令，民之師帥，所使承流而宣化也；
故師帥不賢，則主德不宣，恩澤不流。
今吏既亡教訓於下，或不承用主上之法，暴虐百姓，與奸為市，貧窮孤弱，冤苦失職，甚不稱陛下之意。
是以陰陽錯繆，氛氣棄塞，群生寡遂，黎民未濟，皆長吏不明，使至於此也。
夫長吏多出於郎中、中郎，吏二千石子弟選郎吏，又以富訾，未必賢也。
且古所謂功者，以任官稱職為差，非謂積日累久也。
故小材雖累日，不離於小官；
賢材雖未久，不害為輔佐。
是以有司竭力盡知，務治其業而以赴功。
今則不然。
累日以取貴，積久以致官，是以廉恥貿亂，賢不肖渾淆，未得其真。
臣愚以為使諸列侯、郡守、二千石各擇其吏民之賢者，歲貢各二人以給宿衛，且以觀大臣之能；
所貢賢者有賞，所貢不肖者有罰。
夫如是，諸侯、吏二千石皆盡心於求賢，天下之士可得而官使也。
遍得天下之賢人，則三王之盛易為，而堯、舜之名可及也。
毋以日月為功，實試賢能為上，量材而授官，錄德而定位，則廉恥殊路，賢不肖異處矣。
陛下加惠，寬臣之罪，令勿牽制於文，使得切磋究之，臣敢不盡愚！
於是天子復冊之。
制曰：蓋聞「善言天者必有征於人，善言古者必有驗於今」。
故朕垂問乎天人之應，上嘉唐虞，下悼桀、紂，浸微浸滅浸明浸昌之道，虛心以改。
今子大夫明於陰陽所以造化，習於先聖之道業，然而文采未極，豈惑乎當世之務哉？
條貫靡竟，統紀未終，意朕之不明與？
聽若眩與？
夫三王之教所祖不同，而皆有失，或謂久而不易者道也，意豈異哉？
今子大夫既已著大道之極，陳治亂之端矣，其悉之究之，孰之復之。
《詩》不雲乎，「嗟爾君子，毋常安息，神之聽之，介爾景福。」
朕將親覽焉，子大夫其茂明之。
仲舒復對曰：
臣聞《論語》曰：「有始有卒者，其唯聖人虖！」
今陛下幸加惠，留聽於承學之臣，復下明冊，以切其意，而究盡聖德，非愚臣之所能具也。
前所上對，條貫靡竟，統紀不終，辭不別白，指不分明，此臣淺陋之罪也。
冊曰：「善言天者必有征於人，善言古者必有驗於今。」
臣聞天者群物之祖也。
故遍覆包涵而無所殊，建日月風雨以和之，經陰陽寒暑以成之。
故聖人法天而立道，亦溥愛而亡私，布德施仁以厚之，設誼立禮以導之。
春者天之所以生也，仁者君之所以愛也；
夏者天之所以長也，德者君之所以養也；
霜者天之所以殺也，刑者君之所以罰也。
繇此言之，天人之征，古今之道也。
孔子作《春秋》，上揆之天道，下質諸人情，參之於古，考之於今。
故《春秋》之所譏，災害之所加也；
《春秋》之所惡，怪異之所施也。
書邦家之過，兼災異之變；
以此見人之所為，其美惡之極，乃與天地流通而往來相應，此亦言天之一端也。
古者修教訓之官，務以德善化民，民已大化之後，天下常亡一人之獄矣。
今世廢而不修，亡以化民，民以故棄行誼而死財利，是以犯法而罪多，一歲之獄以萬千數。
以此見古之不可不用也，故《春秋》變古則譏之。
天令之謂命，命非聖人不行；
質樸之謂性，性非教化不成；
人欲之謂情，情非度制不節。
是故王者上謹於承天意，以順命也；
下務明教化民，以成性也；
正法度之宜，別上下之序，以防欲也；
修此三者，而大本舉矣。
人受命於天，固超然異於群生，入有父子兄弟之親，出有君臣上下之誼，會聚相遇，則有耆老長幼之施，粲然有文以相接，歡然有恩以相愛，此人之所以貴也。
生五穀以食之，桑麻以衣之，六畜以養之，服牛乘馬，圈豹檻虎，是其得天之靈，貴於物也。
故孔子曰：「天地之性人為貴。」
明於天性，知自貴於物；
知自貴於物，然後知仁誼；
知仁誼，然後重禮節；
重禮節，然後安處善；
安處善，然後樂循理；
樂循理，然後謂之君之。
故孔子曰「不知命，亡以為君子」，此之謂也。
冊曰：「上嘉唐、虞，下悼桀、紂，浸微浸滅浸明浸昌之道，虛心以改。」
臣聞眾少成多，積小致臣，故聖人莫不以晻致明，以微致顯。
是以堯發於諸侯，舜興乎深山，非一日而顯也，蓋有漸以致之矣。
言出於已，不可塞也；
行發於身，不可掩也。
言行，治之大者，君子之所以動天地也。
故盡小者大，慎微者著。
《詩》云：「惟此文王，小心翼翼。」
胡堯兢兢日行其道，而舜業業日致其孝，善積而名顯，德章而身尊，以其浸明浸昌之道也。
積善在身，猶長日加益，而人不知也；
積惡在身，猶火之銷膏，而人不見也。
非明乎情性察乎流俗者，孰能知之？
此唐、虞之所以得令名，而桀、紂之可為悼懼者也。
夫善惡之相從，如景鄉之應形聲也。
故桀、紂暴謾，讒賊並進，賢知隱伏，惡日顯，國日亂，晏然自以如日在天，終陵夷而大壞。
夫暴逆不仁者，非一日而亡也，亦以漸至，故桀、紂雖亡道，然猶享國十餘年，此其浸微浸滅之道也。
冊曰：「三王之教所祖不同，而皆有失，或謂久而不易者道也，意豈異哉？」
臣聞夫樂而不亂復而不厭者謂之道；
道者萬世之弊，弊者道之失也。
先王之道必有偏而不起之處，故政有眊而不行，舉其偏者以補其弊而已矣。
三王之道所祖不同，非其相反，將以救溢扶衰，所遭之變然也。
故孔子曰：「亡為而治者，其舜乎！」
改正朔，易服色，以順天命而已；
其餘盡循堯道，何更為哉！
故王者有改制之名，亡變道之實。
然夏上忠，殷上敬，周上文者，所繼之救，當用此也。
孔子曰：「殷因於夏禮，所損益可知也；
周因於殷禮，所損益可知也；
其或繼周者，雖百世可知也。」
此言百王之用，以此三者矣。
夏因於虞，而獨不言所損益者，其道如一而所上同也。
道之大原出於天，天不變，道亦不變，是以禹繼舜，舜繼堯，三聖相受而守一道，亡救弊之政也，故不言其所損益也。
繇是觀之，繼治世者其道同，繼亂世者其道變。
今漢繼大亂之後，若宜少損周之文致，用夏之忠者。
陛下有明德嘉道，愍世欲之靡薄，悼王道之不昭，故舉賢良方正之士，論議考問，將欲興仁誼之林德，明帝王之法制，建太平之道也。
臣愚不肖，述所聞，誦所學，道師之言，廑能勿失耳。
若乃論政事之得失，察天下之息耗，此大臣輔佐之職，三公九卿之任，非臣仲舒所能及也，然而臣竊有怪者。
夫古之天下亦今之天下，今之天下亦古之天下，共是天下，古以大治，上下和睦，習俗美盛，不令而行，不禁而止，吏亡奸邪，民亡盜賊，囹圄空虛，德潤草木，澤被四海，鳳皇來集，麒麟來游，以古准今，壹何不相逮之遠也！
安所繆□而陵夷若是？
意者有所失於古之道與？
有所詭於天之理與？
試跡之於古，返之於天，黨可得見乎。
夫天亦有所分予，予之齒者去其角，傅其翼者兩其足，是所受大者不得取小也。
古之所予祿者，不食於力，不動於末，是亦受大者不得取小，與天同意者也。
夫已受大，又取小，天不能足，而況人乎！
此民之所以囂囂苦不足也。
身寵而載高位，家溫而食厚祿，因乘富貴之資力，以與民爭利於下，民安能如之哉！
是故眾其奴婢，多其牛羊，廣其田宅，博其產業，畜其積委，務此而亡已，以迫蹴民，民日削月浸，浸以大窮。
富者奢侈羨溢，貧者窮急愁苦；
窮急愁苦而不上救，則民不樂生；
民不樂生，尚不避死，安能避罪！
此刑罰之所以蕃而奸邪不可勝者也。
故受祿之家，食祿而已，不與民爭業，然後利可均布，而民可家足。
此上天之理，而亦太古之道，天子之所宜法以為制，大夫之所當循以為行也。
故公儀子相魯，之其家見織帛，怒而出其妻，食於捨而茹葵，慍而拔其葵，曰：「吾已食祿，又奪園夫紅女利乎！」
古之賢人君子在列位者皆如是，是故下高其行而從其教，民化其廉而不貪鄙。
及至周室之衰，其卿大夫緩於誼而急於利，亡推讓之風而有爭田之訟。
故詩人疾而刺之，曰：「節彼南山，惟石巖巖，赫赫師尹，民具爾瞻。」
爾好誼，則民鄉仁而俗善；
爾好利，則民好邪而俗敗。
由是觀之，天子大夫者，下民之所視效，遠方之所四面而內望也。
近者視而放之，遠者望而效之，豈可以居賢人之位而為庶人行哉！
夫皇皇求財利常恐乏匱者，庶人之意也；
皇求仁義常恐不能化民者，大夫之意也。
《易》曰：「負且乘，致寇至。」
乘車者君子之位也，負擔著小人之事也，此言居君子之位而為庶人之行者，其患禍必至也。
若居君子之位，當君子之行，則捨公儀休之相魯，亡可為者矣。
《春秋》大一統者，天地之常經，古今之通誼也。
今師異道，人異論，百家殊方，指意不同，是以上亡以持一統；
法制數變，下不知所守。
臣愚以為諸不在六藝之科孔子之術者，皆絕其道，勿使並進。
邪辟之說滅息，然後統紀可一而法度可明，民知所從矣。
對既畢，天子以仲舒為江都相，事易王。
易王，帝兄，素驕，好勇。
仲舒以禮誼匡正，王敬重焉。
久之，王問仲舒曰：「粵王勾踐與大夫洩庸、種、蠡謀伐吳，遂滅之。
孔子稱殷有三仁，寡人亦以為粵有三仁。
桓公決疑於管仲，寡人決疑於君。」
仲舒對曰：「臣愚不足以奉大對。
聞昔者魯君問柳下惠：『吾欲伐齊，何如？』
柳下惠曰：『不可。』
歸而有憂色，曰：『吾聞伐國不問仁人，此言何為至於我哉！』
徒見問耳，且猶羞之，況設詐以伐吳乎？
由此言之，粵本無一仁。
夫仁人者，正其誼不謀其利，明其道不計其功。
是以仲尼之門，五尺之童羞稱五伯，為其先詐力而後仁誼也。
苟為詐而已，故不足稱於大君子之門也。
五伯比於他諸侯為賢，其比三王，猶武夫之與美玉也。」
王曰：「善。」
仲舒治國，以《春秋》災異之變推陰陽所以錯行，故求雨，閉諸陽，縱諸陰，其止雨反是；
行之一國，未嘗不得所欲。
中廢為中大夫。
先是遼東高廟、長陵高園殿災，仲舒居家推說其意，草稿未上，主父偃候仲舒，私見，嫉之，竊其書而奏焉。
上召視諸儒，仲舒弟子呂步舒不知其師書，以為大愚。
於是下仲舒吏，當死，詔赦之，仲舒遂不敢復言災異。
仲舒為人廉直。
是時方外攘四夷，公孫弘治《春秋》不如仲舒，而弘希世用事，位至公卿。
仲舒以弘為從諛，弘嫉之。
膠西王亦上兄也，尤縱恣，數害吏二千石。
弘乃言於上曰：「獨董仲舒可使相膠西王。」
膠西王聞仲舒大儒，善待之。
仲舒恐久獲罪，病免。
凡相兩國，輒事驕王，正身以率下，數上疏諫爭，教令國中，所居而治。
及去位歸居，終不問家產業，以修學著書為事。
仲舒在家，朝廷如有大議，使使者及廷尉張湯就其家而問之，其對皆有明法。
自武帝初立，魏其、武安侯為相而隆儒矣。
及仲舒對冊，推明孔氏，抑黜百家。
立學校之官，州郡舉茂材孝廉，皆自仲舒發之。
年老，以壽終於家，家徙茂陵，子及孫皆以學至大官。
仲舒所著，皆明經術之意，及上疏條教，凡百二十三篇。
而說《春秋》事得失，《聞舉》、《玉杯》、《蕃露》、《清明》、《竹林》之屬，復數十篇，十餘萬言，皆傳於後世。
掇其切當世施朝廷者著於篇。
贊曰：劉向稱：「董仲舒有王佐之材，雖伊、呂亡以加，管、晏之屬，伯者之佐，殆不及也。」
至向子歆以為：「伊、呂乃聖人之耦，王者不得則不興。
故顏淵死，孔子曰『噫！
天喪余。』
唯此一人為能當之，自宰我、子贛、子游、子夏不與焉。
仲舒遭漢承秦滅學之後，《六經》離析，下帷發憤，潛心大業，令後學者有所統壹，為群儒首。
然考其師友淵源所漸，猶未及乎游、夏，而曰管、晏弗及，伊、呂不加，過矣。」
至向曾孫龔，篤論君子也，以歆之言為然。
